storm.summary_layperson: A severe thunderstorm watch is in effect for western Connecticut this afternoon, but the unusually cool Long Island Sound water creates a protective marine layer that typically weakens or splits storms before they reach the cove.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 70°F against 98°F air creates a 27°F gap — a strong marine stable layer that tends to choke incoming convection at the shoreline.
storm.detail_html:
A severe thunderstorm watch is in effect for western portions of the area this afternoon into this evening per SPC and NWS Upton (cited in the OKX Area Forecast Discussion). As of 4:31 PM EDT, radar shows no storm cells approaching the Greenwich Cove area — the watch reflects the potential for development, not actively tracked storms on the immediate doorstep.

Instability is moderate to high: HRRR shows 1,510 J/kg CAPE at the analysis hour, while the RAP model indicates 2,380 J/kg. Bulk wind shear is 18–22 kt WNW across both models. These numbers support organized convection if storms develop. The AFD also notes "isolated to scattered thunderstorms are possible each day through Sunday" with a Slight Risk for severe storms over much of the area.

The key Riverside-specific modifier is the Long Island Sound marine layer. Sound water sits at 70°F against inland air near 97–98°F — a 27°F gap, classified as a strong stable layer. As storms cross the cool Sound water, they lose low-level fuel and often weaken or split at the CT shoreline, leaving the cove with gusty wind but reduced rain and thunder while inland areas a mile or two north get the worst of it. With CAPE below the 3,000 J/kg punch-through gate, this modifier is fully in effect.

Most likely outcome for the cove: any storm that develops to the west or southwest will encounter the marine layer crossing the Sound and arrive diminished — gusty wind and maybe some light rain, while Westchester, White Plains, and areas north of the shoreline take the heavier impact. The cove's risk is real but meaningfully lower than the surrounding inland suburbs.

What would change the call: a cell developing close-in (within 15–20 mi) over the water or from the NNW over land would reduce or bypass the Sound's protective value. Watch for upstream APRS stations to the WNW (Port Chester, Rye) spiking to gust ≥35 kt as the earliest local warning — that would signal a gust front outrunning the reflectivity. An Extreme Heat Warning remains in effect through tonight; the Air Quality Alert expires at 11 PM EDT.

hourly.shift_note: steady gradient, cove may carry NW tendency

wind.tactics_blurb: W–WNW gradient at 7–10 kt through early evening softening to 4–6 kt by 9–10 PM — gradient day means wind holds past sunset but thins. Stay off the CT shore where W/WNW flow holes; favor the left (Sound) side of the course for cleaner pressure.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime C (gradient-thermal hybrid): despite a massive 27°F land–ocean deltaT that should be driving a strong sea breeze, a W–NW synoptic gradient at 9–12 kt is winning the contest. Only 26% of coastal stations are showing the southerly sea-breeze component; the LI north shore ensemble is fully in the W gradient. Authoritative station mean is 9.8 kt, and RYC is reading NW 319° at 11.8 kt gusting 19.5 kt — significantly more northerly than the HRRR-modeled W 260° (a 59° gap). This divergence likely reflects local terrain channeling at the cove head or a residual post-frontal NW influence not yet captured in the model.

HRRR forecast for the race window shows W–WNW gradient at 7–10 kt from 5–7 PM, fading to 4–6 kt by 9 PM. Sunset is ~8:23 PM. For a gradient (not thermal) regime, wind does not collapse at sunset — it fades naturally through the evening. Expect the direction to back gradually from NW toward W as the evening progresses and the local NW surface feature dissipates.

For the cove in W–WNW flow: per Greenwich Cove geography, W–WNW wind channels down from the NYC direction and produces holes near the CT shore (right/north side of the course) with steadier pressure toward the Sound (left/south side). Start left, work toward the center of the course. Avoid overstanding the starboard layline — the right shore underdelivers in this direction. Mid-cove and cove-mouth position will carry the most consistent pressure.

Shift character: steady oscillating around 275–285°, normal gust-lull cycling of ±10°. The competing thermal energy from the 27°F deltaT may produce occasional brief southerly puffs, but the gradient is suppressing them cleanly — no clean oscillation pattern to play tactically. At 7 kt and below (after ~7 PM), the oscillations increase and direction becomes less predictable. Last races of the evening will be in the light-air drifter range (4–6 kt); plan lap count accordingly.

Note: the HRRR/RYC direction gap (59°) is unusually large. If the cove's NW tendency (319°) persists into the race start rather than backing toward HRRR's W 260–278°, the right side carries more tactical value — NW off the cove head has less terrain obstruction on the eastern shore. Watch RYC live before the start. Direction_agreement across the ensemble is 0.62 (borderline fragmented), reflecting the mixed synoptic-thermal environment; treat the directional call as moderate confidence.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) wind observation 76 min stale — over-water sea-breeze depth signal degraded
KFRG (Farmingdale LI) anemometer offline — wind data excluded from ensemble
EW9204 (Valhalla APRS) stale 358 min — discarded from ensemble
Severe thunderstorm watch in effect for western portions of the area; locally mitigated by strong Sound marine layer (27°F delta)
